metadata.title: Build a Brand Metrics vs. Performance Metrics Dashboard with Claude
metadata.purpose: Show students in Nina Harris' Branding and AI course how to build a dual-track measurement plan generator that separates brand metrics from performance metrics and makes the patience gap explicit.
metadata.audience: Students and practitioners in a university branding course with an AI component
sources: Branding and AI, Chapter 13 — Measuring Brand Equity (Nina Harris)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'ClaudeComposerAsk916' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
END: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

metadata.reformat: center-cut, focus-aware; pantry/<bid>-916.* is the human replacement slot; -916 overrides honored

beats.narration_text: Salaam — this is Liam, in for Bear. For Nina Harris' course, Branding and AI, here is the question we are running today: a discount campaign looks like a success on Monday's dashboard and looks like margin erosion in the brand tracking study six months later — because the two metric families answer different questions and most teams only watch one. We are building the plan that watches both.

props.command: Design a dual-track measurement plan for this brand: [BRAND DESCRIPTION]. Include brand metrics track (awareness/consideration/preference/sentiment), performance track (conversion/CAC/ROAS/retention), patience gap estimate, and three NPS limitations.
output: Patience gap: 6 months for DTC category
NPS limits: survivor bias, scale compression, no causal weight

beats.narration_text: Here is what breaks without a dual track. You run a deep-discount campaign. Conversion goes up thirty percent — Monday's dashboard looks great. Six months later your brand tracking study shows price premium erosion and your loyalty cohort is now a deal-seeking cohort. The performance metric said success. The brand metric said damage. Most teams never connect the two because they are not looking at both on the same timeline.

The theory from Chapter Thirteen. Brand metrics — unaided awareness, consideration, preference, association strength, sentiment — measure a multidimensional asset that accumulates slowly over time. Performance metrics — conversion, CAC, ROAS, retention — measure transactions that happen now. The patience gap is the structural delay between investing in brand and seeing it show up in performance: typically three to twelve months depending on category and stage. The Pepsi Kendall Jenner case names the failure: awareness went up and favorability went down simultaneously. Any single number extracted from a multidimensional asset answers one question while leaving the others open.

The centerpiece: this is the dual-track timeline output for a mid-stage DTC brand. Brand metrics track on top in ink: unaided awareness, consideration, and preference — measured quarterly by survey, starting at month zero. Performance metrics track on the bottom in terracotta: conversion, CAC, and ROAS — measured weekly from the dashboard, starting at month six. The patience gap bracket shows that six months separates when the brand investment lands from when the performance signal is expected to move. Source: Branding and AI, Nina Harris, Chapter Thirteen.

Honesty about what this plan does not do. Brand tracking does not require a large budget — unaided awareness and consideration can be measured with small panel surveys, and the plan always includes a low-budget option. And the dual track does not eliminate the attribution problem — knowing that both metrics moved does not tell you why. That is a separate discipline. The plan gives you the monitoring framework; the causal question still requires a holdout test.

artifactLines: Brand tracking does not require a large budget — small panel surveys work.
The dual track does not solve the attribution problem — that needs a holdout.
NPS has specific limits for each brand type — the plan names them explicitly.
The patience gap is an estimate, not a guarantee of when performance will move.

beats.narration_text: Let us recap with Claude. Brand Metrics Dashboard Plan. Four lines.

artifactLines: Brand and performance metrics answer different questions with different clocks.
The patience gap makes the delay between investment and movement explicit.
A dual-track plan names each metric alongside the question it actually answers.
NPS limitations are named so the brand doesn't over-trust a single number.

beats.narration_text: That's the short version. The full video also covers brand metrics dashboard — watch Build a Brand Metrics vs. Performance Metrics Dashboard with Claude for the whole story. The link is right below.
